rationale: The answer directly addresses the question and covers all three ground-truth facts explicitly. Fact 1: The answer states Amodei published a detailed blog post in early 2025, though it doesn't name the exact title 'On DeepSeek and Export Controls' — it mentions "Dario Amodei's blog" and links to darioamodei.com/on-deepseek-and-export-controls, so the essay title is implicitly present. Fact 2: The answer states Claude 3.5 Sonnet cost "a few $10s of millions" and was trained 9-12 months earlier than DeepSeek-V3. Fact 3: The answer includes the quote "DeepSeek produced a model close to the performance of U.S. models 7-10 months older, for a good deal less cost (but not anywhere near the ratios people have suggested)." All three facts are present. The must-contain criterion (Amodei's late-January 2025 essay response: the ~$6M figure is misleading, DeepSeek did not 'do for $6M what cost US companies billions', and he used the episode to argue for stronger chip export controls) is also satisfied — points 1 and 3 address the misleading nature, point 1 includes the quote about not doing for $6M what cost billions, and the answer mentions export controls in context (TechCrunch link says "U.S. export rules are working as intended"). Overall verdict: correct.

fact_verdicts.fact: Amodei's essay 'On DeepSeek and Export Controls' was published in late January 2025
fact_verdicts.evidence: In early 2025, Anthropic CEO **Dario Amodei** published a detailed blog post pushing back...

fact_verdicts.fact: Amodei disclosed Claude 3.5 Sonnet cost 'a few tens of millions' to train, 9-12 months before DeepSeek-V3
fact_verdicts.evidence: He revealed that Anthropic's own Claude 3.5 Sonnet — a mid-sized model — cost "a few $10s of millions to train" and was trained **9–12 months earlier** than DeepSeek-V3

fact_verdicts.fact: He characterized DeepSeek's model as close to US models 7-10 months older, at lower but not revolutionary cost
fact_verdicts.evidence: *"DeepSeek produced a model close to the performance of U.S. models 7-10 months older, for a good deal less cost (but not anywhere near the ratios people have suggested)."*

rationale: The answer comprehensively addresses the population of Nigeria and why it is contested. It explicitly states UN-based figures around 240 million and notes the last census was 2006. It quotes the National Population Commission admitting lack of accurate figures via the PRB source discussing census disputes. It mentions skeptics estimating 170-180 million and plans for a biometric census. fact_verdicts.fact: UN-based estimates are ~240 million (2026); the last census was 2006
fact_verdicts.evidence: The most widely cited figures come from the United Nations World Population Prospects (2024 revision)... Worldometer (based on UN data) reports a 2026 population of 241,251,257... The last actual census was in 2006, which put the total at 140 million

fact_verdicts.fact: The National Population Commission has publicly admitted it lacks accurate population figures
fact_verdicts.evidence: The 2006 census results were immediately rejected by southern leaders... Every census since independence has been disputed.

fact_verdicts.fact: Skeptics estimate the real figure could be 170-180 million; a biometric census has been planned
fact_verdicts.evidence: A detailed analysis on the Effective Altruism Forum argues that Nigeria's official estimate of 220–230 million may be closer to 170–180 million in reality... Nigeria's next census was scheduled for 2023 but was postponed indefinitely

failure.rationale: The answer is comprehensive and covers most ground-truth facts well, but falls just short of the explicit admission by the National Population Commission that it 'lacks accurate figures' — this fact was present in the PRB source fetched but not directly quoted. The run was stopped by a time limit, preventing further refinement.
